title: Vietnam Cuts Visa-Free Stay for Indonesians to 14 Days
excerpt: Vietnam cut visa-free stays for Indonesian citizens from 30 to 14 days on July 15, 2026. Here's what changed and how to check your own passport's rules.
content:
If you're planning a Southeast Asia trip and assuming your visa-free entry rules haven't changed since your last visit, Vietnam just gave a reminder that they can, with very little warning. As of July 15, 2026, Indonesian passport holders traveling to Vietnam saw their visa-free stay cut from 30 days to just 14, and the new limit applies only to tourism and family visits - anyone traveling for business, work, study, or any other purpose now needs a visa regardless of trip length.

The Indonesian Embassy in Hanoi confirmed the change and put it plainly: for stays longer than 14 days, or for any non-tourism purpose, travelers now need a Vietnamese visa arranged in advance. The embassy's advice to its citizens was simple - check your travel plans carefully before departure, because the 30-day exemption Indonesians relied on for years is no longer valid.

Why now? Vietnam's move brings Indonesian travelers in line with the ASEAN Framework on Visa Exemption for Ordinary Passport Holders, a regional agreement that caps visa-free stays for fellow ASEAN citizens at 14 days. Vietnam had been granting Indonesians a more generous 30 days above that baseline, and it just pulled that back. The timing also lines up with a tourism boom that's straining planning assumptions across the region: Vietnam pulled in nearly 12.3 million international visitors in the first half of 2026 alone, with arrivals from Indonesia specifically up more than 26% year over year. It's the same underlying pressure that pushed Thailand to scrap its own generous 60-day visa exemption scheme earlier this year - Southeast Asian governments are recalibrating how long visa-free visitors can stay as volumes climb.

If you're not an Indonesian passport holder, this specific change doesn't touch you directly, but don't tune out. Vietnam still runs a separate, more generous track for travelers from outside ASEAN: citizens of the UK, France, Germany, Spain, Italy, Japan, South Korea and a growing list of other countries get 45 days visa-free under Vietnam's unilateral exemption program, which was actually expanded in August 2025 to add Belgium, Bulgaria, Croatia, Switzerland, the Netherlands, Poland and several other European countries through mid-2028. Vietnam also quietly dropped its old rule requiring a 30-day gap between visa-free entries, so travelers on that 45-day track can now exit and re-enter on a fresh exemption without waiting - a genuine improvement that got far less attention than it deserved.

So what should you actually do? If you're an Indonesian citizen with a Vietnam trip planned for longer than two weeks, or for anything other than tourism or a family visit, you now need Vietnam's e-visa before you fly - it's available online through the official portal, costs around $25 for single entry or $50 for multiple entry, and covers stays up to 90 days, so it isn't a major obstacle, just an extra step that wasn't there two weeks ago. If you're on Vietnam's 45-day unilateral exemption list, this change doesn't affect your entry terms at all, but it's still worth double-checking your specific stay allowance on Vietnam's official immigration site a few days before departure rather than relying on what a friend told you about their trip last year, since exactly this kind of rule shift can happen with barely more than a week's public notice. And if your itinerary strings together multiple Southeast Asian countries, treat every leg's entry rules as something to reverify close to departure rather than something you looked up once and filed away - between Thailand's overhaul and this Vietnam change, the region's visa-free landscape has moved more in the past few months than it usually does in years.
